---
title: "Getting Started with RBiblioSynth"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Getting Started with RBiblioSynth}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

# Getting Started with RBiblioSynth

This vignette provides a comprehensive introduction to RBiblioSynth, a modular R framework for bibliometric analysis and systematic review reporting.

## Installation

```r
# From GitHub (development version)
devtools::install_github("yourusername/RBiblioSynth")

# Load the package
library(RBiblioSynth)
```

## Quick Start

### 1. Load Data

RBiblioSynth supports multiple bibliographic databases:

```r
# Define data sources
sources <- list(
  scopus = list(
    file = "data/scopus.bib",
    db = "scopus",
    format = "bibtex"
  ),
  wos = list(
    file = "data/wos.bib", 
    db = "wos",
    format = "bibtex"
  )
)

# Or use the sample dataset
data(biblio_sample)
```

### 2. Run M0 Data Orchestrator

```r
# Run M0 to load, merge, and organize data
m0_result <- run_m0(sources)

# Access merged data
bib_data <- m0_get_bib_data(m0_result)

# Access organized data
countries <- m0_get(m0_result, "countries")
authors <- m0_get(m0_result, "authors")
annual <- m0_get(m0_result, "annual")
```

### 3. Run M1 Main Information

```r
# Run comprehensive main information analysis
m1_result <- run_m1(bib_data)

# View results
print(m1_result$data$overview)
print(m1_result$data$authors)
print(m1_result$data$bradford)

# Access plots
m1_result$artifacts$plots$overview$m1_overview_bar
m1_result$artifacts$plots$lotka$m1_lotka_scatter
```

### 4. Run M2 Annual Production

```r
# Run annual production analysis
m2_result <- run_m2(annual)

# View growth model comparison
print(m2_result$data$growth_models$comparison)

# View best model
print(m2_result$data$growth_models$best_model)

# Access forecast
print(m2_result$data$forecasting$arima)
```

### 5. Run M3 Countries

```r
# Run country-level analysis
m3_result <- run_m3(bib_data)

# View spatial statistics
print(m3_result$data$spatial$morans_i)

# View economic correlation
print(m3_result$data$economic)

# View collaboration indices
print(m3_result$data$collaboration_indices)
```

## Advanced Features

### Bootstrap Confidence Intervals

All key metrics include bootstrap confidence intervals:

```r
# Compute h-index with bootstrap CI
citations <- c(10, 25, 5, 15, 30, 8, 12, 45, 3, 18)

h_index <- function(x) {
  sorted <- sort(x, decreasing = TRUE)
  max(which(sorted >= seq_along(sorted)))
}

ci <- bootstrap_ci(citations, h_index, R = 1000, method = "bca")
print(ci)
```

### Growth Model Comparison

```r
# Compare 30+ growth models
data(annual_sample)
m2_result <- run_m2(annual_sample)

# View all models
print(m2_result$data$growth_models$comparison)

#          model      AIC      BIC  R_squared
# 1        bass   245.32   251.45     0.9845
# 2    gompertz   238.91   244.12     0.9891
# 3     weibull   241.56   246.78     0.9873
# 4    richards   235.42   240.63     0.9912
```

### Spatial Statistics

```r
# Moran's I for spatial autocorrelation
m3_result$data$spatial$morans_i

# LISA clusters
m3_result$data$spatial$lisa$clusters

# Getis-Ord Gi* hot spots
m3_result$data$spatial$getis_ord$hotspots
```

### QAP Network Test

```r
# Test correlation between collaboration and citation networks
collab_matrix <- m3_result$data$collaboration_indices$matrix
citation_matrix <- compute_citation_correlation_matrix(bib_data)

qap_result <- m3_compute_qap_test(
  collab_matrix,
  citation_matrix,
  n_permutations = 1000
)

print(qap_result$observed_correlation)  # 0.45
print(qap_result$p_value_two_tailed)    # 0.002
print(qap_result$interpretation)
# "The networks show a moderate positive correlation (r = 0.450), 
# which is significant (p = 0.0020)."
```

## Visualization

### Treemaps

```r
# Country production treemap
treemap <- render_m1_treemap(m1_result$data, type = "countries")
print(treemap$plots$treemap)
```

### Sankey Diagrams

```r
# Collaboration flows
sankey <- render_m3_sankey(m3_result$data, type = "country")
print(sankey$plots$sankey)
```

### Three-Field Plots

```r
# Authors - Keywords - Sources
three_field <- render_m1_three_field(
  bib_data,
  fields = list(
    field1 = list(name = "Authors", column = "AU"),
    field2 = list(name = "Keywords", column = "DE"),
    field3 = list(name = "Sources", column = "SO")
  )
)
print(three_field$plots$three_field)
```

## PRISMA Flow Diagrams

```r
# Define PRISMA specification
prisma <- list(
  title = "Systematic Review Title",
  identification = list(
    records_database = 1250,
    records_other = 45,
    duplicates_removed = 180
  ),
  screening = list(
    records_screened = 1115,
    excluded_screening = 780
  ),
  eligibility = list(
    fulltext_assessed = 335,
    excluded_fulltext = 195
  ),
  included = list(
    studies_included = 140
  )
)

# Generate PRISMA diagram
m0_render_prisma_diagram(prisma)
```

## Export

```r
# Export to JSON
write_json_artifact(m1_result$data$overview, "output/m1_overview.json")

# Export plots
export_plot_artifact(m1_result$artifacts$plots$overview$m1_overview_bar, 
                     "output/overview", width =10, height = 8)
```

## Statistical Tests

Each module includes formal hypothesis testing:

```r
# M1 Hypothesis tests
m1_result$data$hypotheses

# M2 Hypothesis tests
m2_result$data$hypotheses

# M3 Hypothesis tests  
m3_result$data$hypotheses
```

## Next Steps

1. Explore the [Module Reference](../reference/index.html) for detailed function documentation
2. Read the [Advanced Analysis vignette](advanced-analysis.html) for time series and spatial methods
3. Check [Visualization Examples](visualization-examples.html) for publication-ready figures

## Citation

If you use RBiblioSynth in your research, please cite:

```bibtex
@article{rbibliosynth2026,
  title = {RBiblioSynth: A Comprehensive R Framework for Bibliometric Analysis},
  author = {Mayorga},
  journal = {SoftwareX},
  year = {2026}
}
```
